title: Tensor-based second-order causal discovery
abstract: Causal discovery seeks to uncover the causal dependencies among variables. For this purpose, we propose an algorithm called Tensor-based Second-order Causal Discovery (TSCD). Its input is a tensor obtained from the covariance matrices of observational and interventional data. Assuming the causal dependencies follow a linear structural equation model on a directed acyclic graph (DAG), TSCD outputs the DAG and the functions on its edges, requiring only that the noise variables are uncorrelated. We also implement a version of the approach for nonlinear models. Our focus on second-order statistics (via the covariance matrices) is motivated by their statistical and computational efficiency relative to higher-order moments, their identifiability relative to first-order statistics, and that they work regardless of whether the variables are Gaussian. We show that TSCD has identifiable causal order and parameters from a number of interventions that is logarithmic in the number of variables. Experiments show that TSCD is robust to noise, competitive with existing methods, and scales to hundreds of variables.

objective.answer: The primary objective of the paper is to propose an algorithm called Tensor-based Second-order Causal Discovery (TSCD) to uncover causal dependencies among variables using covariance matrices from observational and interventional data.
objective.evidence: For this purpose, we propose an algorithm called Tensor-based Second-order Causal Discovery (TSCD). Its input is a tensor obtained from the covariance matrices of observational and interventional data.

knowledge_gap.answer: Existing methods for causal discovery often rely on higher-order statistics or make assumptions about noise variables that limit their applicability. There is a need for methods that can efficiently use second-order statistics to identify causal structures.
knowledge_gap.evidence: Second-order statistics are practical in applications where per-context sample sizes are too small to reliably estimate full distributions. We see that such scenarios limit the accuracy of approaches that use non-Gaussianity assumptions, such as Linear Non-Gaussian Acyclic Model (LiNGAM).

answer: TSCD uses only second-order statistics, which are more efficient and applicable in scenarios with Gaussian noise variables.
The method constructs a tensor from covariance matrices, whose decomposition encodes causal relationships and intervention patterns.
TSCD provides identifiability guarantees with a logarithmic number of interventions relative to the number of variables.
evidence: TSCD is second-order because it takes as input only the covariance matrices of the data.
TSCD is tensor-based because it works by constructing a tensor from the covariance matrices, whose decomposition encodes the causal relationships and intervention patterns.
We show that TSCD has identifiable causal order and parameters from a number of interventions that is logarithmic in the number of variables.

inspirational_papers.answer:
- Shimizu et al. (2006) A linear non-Gaussian acyclic model for causal discovery. (Methodological precursors)
- Peters et al. (2017) Elements of causal inference: foundations and learning algorithms. (Experimental baselines)
inspirational_papers.evidence: Second-order statistics are practical in applications where per-context sample sizes are too small to reliably estimate full distributions. We see that such scenarios limit the accuracy of approaches that use non-Gaussianity assumptions, such as Linear Non-Gaussian Acyclic Model (LiNGAM) [SHHK06].

steps.step: Construct a tensor from covariance matrices across contexts.
steps.input: Covariance matrices from observational and interventional data.
steps.output: A tensor that encodes causal relationships and intervention patterns.
steps.evidence: We construct the tensor that is the core of our identifiability proof and algorithm. It is obtained by combining precision matrices across contexts.

steps.step: Perform tensor decomposition to identify causal order and parameters.
steps.input: The constructed tensor from covariance matrices.
steps.output: Identified causal order and model parameters.
steps.evidence: The decomposition of the precision tensor T is a CP decomposition: it writes a tensor as a sum of outer products.

steps.step: Use subspace-proximity score to identify root nodes.
steps.output: Root nodes of the causal graph.
steps.evidence: We use a greedy procedure to identify root nodes from the precision tensor. Recall that root nodes are those satisfying ei ⊗bi ∈A.

tools.name: Tensor Decomposition
tools.description: Used to decompose the tensor constructed from covariance matrices to identify causal relationships.
tools.evidence: The decomposition of the precision tensor T is a CP decomposition: it writes a tensor as a sum of outer products.

benchmark_datasets.name: Flow-cytometry Dataset
benchmark_datasets.data_description: Single-cell protein phosphorylation data across experimental conditions.
benchmark_datasets.usage: Used to evaluate the graph recovered by the method.
benchmark_datasets.evidence: We evaluate our method on the flow-cytometry dataset [SPP+05], which records single-cell protein phosphorylation.

evaluation_metrics.name: F1 Score
evaluation_metrics.purpose: Measures the accuracy of edge recovery in the causal graph.
evaluation_metrics.application: Used to evaluate the performance of the method in recovering the causal graph.
evaluation_metrics.evidence: Performance is evaluated using the relative Frobenius error, for methods estimating Λ, the F1 score for edge recovery.

evaluation_metrics.name: Relative Frobenius Error
evaluation_metrics.purpose: Measures the error in estimating the adjacency matrix.
evaluation_metrics.application: Used to compare the accuracy of the estimated adjacency matrix to the true matrix.
evaluation_metrics.evidence: Performance is evaluated using the relative Frobenius error, for methods estimating Λ.

methods.name: Hypothesis or Idea Generation
methods.description: The system produces candidate hypotheses or new research ideas from prior knowledge or external input.
methods.evidence: We propose a new algorithm for causal discovery, which we call Tensor-based Second-order Causal Discovery (TSCD).

methods.name: Experimental design generation
methods.description: The approach includes producing experimental protocols, configurations, or evaluation strategies.
methods.evidence: We evaluate TSCD on synthetic linear and nonlinear structural equation models, comparing against causal discovery baselines.

areas.name: Applied Sciences & Engineering
areas.description: The paper develops a method for causal discovery applicable to various scientific fields.
areas.evidence: Causal discovery is a fundamental problem in biology, neuroscience, economics, public health and machine learning.

performance_summary.summary: TSCD achieves high accuracy and computational efficiency, performing comparably to LiNGAM in non-Gaussian settings and outperforming baselines when there is Gaussian noise.
performance_summary.evidence: Our method achieves high accuracy and computational efficiency, performing comparably to LiNGAM in non-Gaussian settings and outperforming baselines when there is Gaussian noise.

baselines.name: LiNGAM
baselines.description: A method for causal discovery assuming non-Gaussian noise.
baselines.evidence: Our method achieves high accuracy and computational efficiency, performing comparably to LiNGAM in non-Gaussian settings.

baselines.name: GES
baselines.description: A score-based method for learning a DAG.
baselines.evidence: We compare three classes of methods: Interventional methods (ours, GIES, IGSP), Observational methods with adjacency matrix output (LiNGAM, SortRegress, NOTEARS), Observational methods with graph output (GES, PC).

limitations.name: Assumption of Perfect Interventions
limitations.description: The method assumes perfect interventions, which may not hold in real-world data.
limitations.evidence: Our method relies on assumptions such as perfect interventions and uncorrelated noise, which may be violated in real-world data.

limitations.name: Uncorrelated Noise Assumption
limitations.description: The method assumes noise variables are uncorrelated, which may not be true in practice.
limitations.evidence: Our method relies on assumptions such as perfect interventions and uncorrelated noise, which may be violated in real-world data.

future_directions.name: Handle Other Types of Interventions
future_directions.description: Extend the framework to handle noise-shift and soft interventions.
future_directions.evidence: Future directions include extending the framework to handle other types of interventions and latent confounding.

future_directions.name: Incorporate Higher-order Statistics
future_directions.description: Explore the use of first or higher-order statistics in the method.
future_directions.evidence: Incorporating first or higher-order statistics is another interesting direction for future work.
